Name: RateDistortionModelCompression
Title: Rate-Distortion Analysis for Adaptive Model Compression in Deep Learning
Short Hypothesis: Model compression in deep learning can be optimized using rate-distortion theory to achieve a better trade-off between model size and performance, leading to improved population risk when the compression reduces the generalization error more than it increases the empirical risk.
Related Work: Existing work has shown that model compression can improve population risk by reducing overfitting (Bu et al., 2019, 2021). However, these studies primarily focus on theoretical bounds and specific compression techniques. Our proposal aims to develop a more adaptive compression framework that dynamically adjusts the compression level based on the task-relevant information, ensuring optimal trade-offs between model size and performance.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to model compression in deep learning using rate-distortion theory. Our method adaptively compresses models by dynamically adjusting the compression level based on the task-relevant information. We derive theoretical bounds on the trade-off between model size and performance, and we show how to optimize this trade-off to achieve improved population risk. Through experiments with neural networks, we demonstrate that our adaptive compression framework can achieve better performance than static compression methods while maintaining or reducing model size.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the rate-distortion trade-off for different compression techniques (pruning, quantization) on a variety of deep learning models (e.g., ResNet, VGG) using datasets like CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
Experiments.Method: Use mutual information and entropy estimators to quantify the information content of the model's representations. Apply rate-distortion theory to derive the optimal compression level for each model and dataset combination.
Experiments.Metrics: Compression ratio, test accuracy, generalization error, and empirical risk.

Experiments.Description: Compare the performance of adaptive compression with static compression methods (e.g., fixed pruning thresholds, uniform quantization) on the same models and datasets.
Experiments.Method: Implement both adaptive and static compression methods and measure their performance in terms of model size and test accuracy. Use the rate-distortion framework to analyze the trade-offs.
Experiments.Metrics: Compression ratio, test accuracy, generalization error, and empirical risk.

Experiments.Description: Analyze the impact of adaptive compression on the population risk of the models.
Experiments.Method: Derive theoretical bounds on the population risk improvement due to adaptive compression. Validate these bounds through experiments by comparing the population risk of compressed models with their uncompressed counterparts.
Experiments.Metrics: Population risk, generalization error, and empirical risk.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds derived may not hold for all model architectures and datasets, requiring extensive empirical validation.
The adaptive compression framework may introduce additional computational overhead during the training process.
The choice of distortion measure and the estimation of mutual information can be challenging and may affect the accuracy of the results.
